title: Chain Migration

jokes.text: Well, stupider.
jokes.setup: George Sr. wants to find Buster before he does something stupid
jokes.punchline: The correction to 'stupider' - implying Buster's escape attempt was already stupid
jokes.explanation: Perfect character moment - George Sr.'s casual cruelty toward Buster delivered with comedic timing. The self-correction is both economical and revealing.

jokes.text: My left, your right. Your left, my right. My right, your right. My right, my left.
jokes.setup: Two people chained together trying to coordinate movement on a tandem bike
jokes.punchline: The complete confusion and inability to coordinate basic directions
jokes.explanation: Standard physical comedy setup, gets the job done but isn't particularly inventive. The escalation is solid.

jokes.text: Although, it is the only way to ride one of those... when you're chained to a guy.
jokes.setup: The couple struggling with coordination on a tandem bike
jokes.punchline: Narrator's deadpan observation that being chained together is actually the only way to ride a tandem bike
jokes.explanation: Classic Arrested Development narrator wit - finding the absurd logic in the situation. The timing and structure are excellent.

jokes.text: Justin Be-Bear is seven... that was, like, seven years ago.
jokes.setup: Lucille trying to remember when she last went to Build-A-Bear
jokes.punchline: The ridiculous pun name 'Justin Be-Bear' and calculating its age like a real person
jokes.explanation: Excellent character writing - only Lucille would name a Build-A-Bear and track its age. The pun is groan-worthy in the best way.

jokes.text: Drink-A-Juice and there's Paint-A-Plate and Build-A-Bear
jokes.setup: Listing Buster's favorite stores at the mall
jokes.punchline: All the cutesy mall store names that appeal to someone with Buster's mental age
jokes.explanation: Good character consistency showing Buster's juvenile interests, though the joke pattern is becoming predictable.

jokes.text: Now, admittedly, he's not 28 anymore, but... No, you're right.
jokes.setup: Discussing whether adult Buster would still go to these childish stores
jokes.punchline: George Sr. immediately agreeing that Buster acts younger than 28
jokes.explanation: Great character moment showing both parents' shared understanding of Buster's immaturity. The quick agreement is perfectly timed.

jokes.text: But don't you think he would've smelled me by now? Oh, good point. Maybe the Popcorn Plantation is masking your scent.
jokes.setup: George Sr. wondering why Buster hasn't found them
jokes.punchline: The implication that George Sr. has a distinctive bad smell that Buster tracks
jokes.explanation: Disturbing family dynamic played for laughs - the idea that Buster tracks George Sr. by scent like an animal. Lucille's matter-of-fact response sells it.

jokes.text: Didn't I used to have one? Yes, but it was real.
jokes.setup: George Sr. looking at a Build-A-Bear and remembering having one
jokes.punchline: Lucille's cutting reminder that his was a real bear (implying wealth/extravagance)
jokes.explanation: Perfect encapsulation of the family's former wealth and current situation. Lucille's deadpan delivery of an absurd fact is peak character writing.

jokes.text: Can you make one of those from a wallet photo?
jokes.setup: Looking at Build-A-Bear and wanting to make one of Buster
jokes.punchline: The ridiculous idea of creating a stuffed animal from a wallet photo
jokes.explanation: Good absurdist humor showing George Sr.'s disconnect from reality and how Build-A-Bear actually works.

jokes.text: Unfortunately, the speed of the printer made it very clear why Two Hour Teeth went out of business.
jokes.setup: Michael printing something on an extremely slow printer
jokes.punchline: The reveal that this slow printer explains why 'Two Hour Teeth' (a dental business) failed
jokes.explanation: Brilliant logical connection - if your printer is this slow, of course your dental practice promising quick service would fail. Classic AD world-building humor.

jokes.text: You know, if you add water to the freeze-dried ground beef... it tastes just as good as any hamburger I've ever had underwater.
jokes.setup: Tobias trying to make freeze-dried food sound appetizing
jokes.punchline: The bizarre comparison to hamburgers eaten underwater
jokes.explanation: Peak Tobias - taking a simple statement and making it inexplicably weird. The underwater hamburger comparison is so random it's genius.

jokes.text: Shh. That was meant to last all week. Oh. Oh, duh.
jokes.setup: Lindsay shushing Tobias about the food situation
jokes.punchline: Tobias's delayed realization that he just ate their entire week's food supply
jokes.explanation: Standard Tobias obliviousness. The delayed 'duh' is good character work but not groundbreaking.

jokes.text: The leg chains are all mixed up with the bike chain.
jokes.setup: George Sr. and Lucille at hardware store with their chained situation
jokes.punchline: The matter-of-fact diagnosis of their ridiculous predicament
jokes.explanation: Good deadpan humor from a minor character treating the absurd situation as routine hardware store business.

jokes.text: Am I crazy, or am I smelling popcorn and Chanel No. 5 butter?
jokes.setup: George Sr. detecting familiar scents at the mall
jokes.punchline: The absurd combination of luxury perfume with movie theater butter
jokes.explanation: Clever wordplay mixing high-end perfume with cheap mall food. Good sensory comedy that fits George Sr.'s character.

jokes.text: These are stage manacles. We were in a play.
jokes.setup: Hardware clerk assumes they're real criminals
jokes.punchline: George Sr.'s weak lie about being in a play
jokes.explanation: Standard embarrassed excuse-making. The implausibility of George Sr. in theater adds some character humor.

jokes.text: Have you guys even tried, uh, just unclamping? Are you kidding me? We didn't even check.
jokes.setup: The couple has been struggling with chains for the entire episode
jokes.punchline: They never tried the simple solution of just opening them
jokes.explanation: Great payoff to the running gag. The audience realizes along with the characters how stupid they've been. Excellent structure.

jokes.text: That would've saved time when we were trying to get the pants over our heads.
jokes.setup: Realizing they weren't actually chained together
jokes.punchline: The disturbing mental image of them struggling to put pants on while 'chained'
jokes.explanation: Good visual comedy callback to their earlier struggles. The mental image is appropriately disturbing and funny.

jokes.text: They're looking for two guys who are manacled together on the run. The Inside-Outside Bandits.
jokes.setup: Discussing their fugitive status
jokes.punchline: The ridiculous name 'Inside-Outside Bandits' based on their earlier argument about outdoor preferences
jokes.explanation: Excellent callback that creates a memorable bandit name from their personality clash. Good layered writing.

jokes.text: I could sniff out Mother somehow, but she wouldn't want me. Maybe I'm better in prison. I'm no good on my own.
jokes.setup: Buster considering his options as a 'fugitive'
jokes.punchline: His pathetic self-assessment and preference for incarceration over independence
jokes.explanation: Perfect Buster characterization - infantile, self-aware about his inadequacy, and preferring institutional care. Dark but earned through character.

jokes.text: Inside. Outside. Inside.
jokes.setup: The ongoing argument about indoor vs outdoor preferences
jokes.punchline: Buster and Oscar having the same argument George Sr. and Lucille had
jokes.explanation: Good running gag that shows how family traits repeat. The parallel is well-executed.

jokes.text: We didn't uncheck 'Make Background Tongue.' I mean, why do they even call it Two Hour Teeth?
jokes.setup: The printer problem continues to affect their wall blueprint
jokes.punchline: More confusion about the defunct dental practice name while dealing with printer settings
jokes.explanation: Decent callback that continues the printer/dental practice joke. Serviceable but not exceptional.

jokes.text: Feels like we're outside. It feels like we're inside.
jokes.setup: They find a solution to their indoor/outdoor disagreement
jokes.punchline: They're in a tent display that satisfies both preferences simultaneously
jokes.explanation: Clever solution to their ongoing conflict. The visual gag of them in a store display is well-crafted.

jokes.text: You're looking at a boy who faked a coma for two weeks to get out of a spelling bee.
jokes.setup: Oscar asking if Buster can pretend to be a mannequin
jokes.punchline: Buster's ridiculous claim about faking a coma for a trivial reason
jokes.explanation: Brilliant Buster moment - the extreme lengths he'd go to avoid minor challenges. Perfectly captures his character and is highly quotable.

jokes.text: That was to get out of testifying. No, that was a three-month coma.
jokes.setup: Oscar correcting Buster about the coma reasons
jokes.punchline: The revelation that Buster has faked multiple comas for different evasions, with increasing duration
jokes.explanation: Perfect escalation - not one but multiple fake comas, each for progressively serious situations. Dark and hilarious character revelation.

jokes.text: If something happens out there, and I get caught and I don't come back, I just want to tell you that... this... this... being with you has been... ah, it's been... I cherish it. My son.
jokes.setup: Oscar preparing to leave the tent display for food
jokes.punchline: The overly dramatic declaration of love for what's essentially a shopping trip
jokes.explanation: Good cringe humor showing Oscar's need for connection and tendency toward melodrama. The father-son dynamic is touching and funny.

jokes.text: It makes you not even want to do business with a hostile nation.
jokes.setup: George Sr. complaining about problems with Chinese investors
jokes.punchline: His casual reference to China as a 'hostile nation' while trying to do business with them
jokes.explanation: Perfect George Sr. - casual prejudice mixed with business incompetence. The contradiction is played completely straight.

jokes.text: How much fun would that be? What are you, nuts?
jokes.setup: George Sr. enthusiastically suggesting they rent a tandem bike
jokes.punchline: Lucille's immediate rejection given their recent chaining disaster
jokes.explanation: Good character consistency - George Sr.'s oblivious optimism vs. Lucille's practicality. Nice callback to opening scene.

jokes.text: They throw these in with the posters. Actually, he's not technically free. He still has to officially sign out from prison and pay their $75 process fee.
jokes.setup: Oscar showing proof that Buster isn't being hunted
jokes.punchline: The mundane bureaucratic detail that keeps the charade going
jokes.explanation: Good absurdist humor about prison bureaucracy. The $75 fee is a perfectly mundane detail that maintains the plot.

jokes.text: I'm fighting against the competition. What, is she dating? No, I'm talking about Buster.
jokes.setup: George Sr. talking about competition for Lucille's affection
jokes.punchline: The reveal that his romantic rival is his own son
jokes.explanation: Great misdirection and disturbing family dynamic. The idea of competing romantically with Buster for Lucille is perfectly twisted.

jokes.text: And we're really bonding over this 'we miss our son' bullshit.
jokes.setup: George Sr. explaining his strategy with Lucille
jokes.punchline: Casually admitting he's faking concern for his own son
jokes.explanation: Perfectly dark George Sr. moment - casual admission of using his son's crisis for marital manipulation

jokes.text: What am I doing, apologizing to a mannequin that looks like... Buster?
jokes.setup: Tobias accidentally entering the wrong tent and apologizing
jokes.punchline: His realization that the 'mannequin' is actually Buster
jokes.explanation: Decent payoff to the mannequin setup. The recognition moment is well-structured but predictable.

jokes.text: Seems like we were just celebrating the second of July. We're already talking about December 19.
jokes.setup: Discussion about Santa Ski Village replacing tent city
jokes.punchline: Tobias's confused reference to 'second of July' instead of Fourth of July
jokes.explanation: Classic Tobias malapropism. His inability to get basic American holidays right is perfectly in character.

jokes.text: No, you have to be 55 and older. Well, that sounds like a challenge to any family that wasn't a ragtag troupe of folks whom I wish to become actors.
jokes.setup: Discussing age requirements for the Spotted Palm retirement community
jokes.punchline: Tobias's convoluted way of saying they could all pretend to be old actors
jokes.explanation: Pure Tobias - overly complicated sentence structure that barely makes sense. The actor obsession is perfectly integrated.

jokes.text: Have I asked before? Every month. Oh, no. That can't be right. Right. See? You always say 'right.'
jokes.setup: Stan with supposed memory issues asking about his condition
jokes.punchline: Maeby's fake pattern recognition that she's making up on the spot
jokes.explanation: Clever manipulation scene where Maeby creates false patterns. The meta aspect of pointing out speech patterns is well-executed.

jokes.text: No, they hadn't. In fact, this had only happened a few hours earlier.
jokes.setup: Maeby claiming they've discussed this many times
jokes.punchline: Narrator's direct contradiction revealing Maeby's lie
jokes.explanation: Great narrator intervention that reveals the deception to the audience. Economical and effective dramatic irony.

jokes.text: You also always say 'old man.'
jokes.setup: Continuing to establish fake patterns in Stan's behavior
jokes.punchline: Maeby inventing another 'recurring' phrase that just happened
jokes.explanation: Good continuation of the manipulation. Shows Maeby's quick thinking and dishonesty in real time.

jokes.text: A real lady lets it itch. She doesn't jam two fingers...
jokes.setup: Maeby apparently adjusting herself inappropriately
jokes.punchline: The awkward advice about ladylike behavior while doing the opposite
jokes.explanation: Standard cringe humor about personal habits. Not particularly clever but fits Maeby's crude character.

jokes.text: Hi, I'm Mrs. Featherbottom. Thank you for being a friend. I'm Bev. Traveled down a road and back again. And I'm Mrs. Bettywhite.
jokes.setup: The family disguising themselves as elderly women to get into the retirement community
jokes.punchline: The terrible old lady names and Golden Girls theme song performance
jokes.explanation: Great callback to Tobias's nanny disguise. The Golden Girls reference and terrible names are well-executed visual comedy.

jokes.text: We're just three... 56-year-old women looking for a place to rest our weary asses.
jokes.setup: Tobias trying to sound like an elderly woman
jokes.punchline: His inappropriate language that breaks character immediately
jokes.explanation: Good Tobias moment where his attempt at acting fails immediately. The crude language from 'elderly lady' is funny.

jokes.text: In the Chinese culture, a good marriage portends a good business relationship.
jokes.setup: George Sr. trying to convince Lucille to practice being a good couple
jokes.punchline: His completely made-up cultural 'fact' to serve his agenda
jokes.explanation: Perfect George Sr. - making up authoritative-sounding nonsense to manipulate. The fake cultural knowledge is very believable.

jokes.text: I think I can eat a piece of fish without giving away how I loathe you.
jokes.setup: George Sr. worried about their performance as a couple
jokes.punchline: Lucille's casual admission of hatred delivered matter-of-factly
jokes.explanation: Classic Lucille - brutal honesty delivered with perfect deadpan. The casual cruelty is both shocking and hilarious.

jokes.text: I helped her take down all your pictures.
jokes.setup: Dusty recognizing George Sr. from somewhere
jokes.punchline: The reveal that he helped Lucille remove George Sr.'s photos
jokes.explanation: Good emasculation moment for George Sr. The casual way Dusty mentions helping with the breakup is effective.

jokes.text: Those steps are just for the feet of the Bay Boys. Get back in the ball, dude.
jokes.setup: George Sr. trying to leave the beach
jokes.punchline: Being forced back into the humiliating beach ball to leave
jokes.explanation: Good physical comedy callback. The territorial surf gang behavior is absurd but consistent.

jokes.text: Oh, you served in Vietnam? Surfed. I surfed in Vietnam. Two group tours.
jokes.setup: George Sr. assuming Dusty was a Vietnam veteran
jokes.punchline: The reveal that he was just a surf tourist on group tours
jokes.explanation: Excellent misdirection and deflation of Dusty's supposed expertise. The contrast between military service and surf tourism is perfect.

jokes.text: We went for sushi last night, and he never touched his fork. Well, a little bit for the rice. It's the hard part. But not when the guy was looking.
jokes.setup: Lucille defending Dusty's cultural knowledge
jokes.punchline: Dusty's admission that he secretly used a fork and avoided detection
jokes.explanation: Good character humor showing Dusty's fake expertise. The sneaky fork use while pretending cultural sensitivity is well-observed.

jokes.text: If you think that I enjoy being put in the position of ridiculing you, intimidating you, emasculating you, you're wrong, little guy. Wrong.
jokes.setup: Dusty claiming he doesn't enjoy putting George Sr. down
jokes.punchline: The obvious contradiction while actively ridiculing and emasculating him
jokes.explanation: Good ironic humor where Dusty does exactly what he claims not to enjoy. The 'little guy' addition is perfectly condescending.

jokes.text: That doesn't look like a mouth to you?
jokes.setup: Michael showing them the wall blueprint
jokes.punchline: Lucille's attempt to make it look like something innocent
jokes.explanation: Decent attempt at misdirection. Links back to the printer problem but isn't particularly strong.

jokes.text: There's Chinese Romneys? No, the only Romneys are the Mexican Romneys. Well, and the regular Romneys.
jokes.setup: Confusion about different types of Romneys
jokes.punchline: The ridiculous taxonomy of Romney varieties
jokes.explanation: Good absurdist humor about political families. The classification system is funny but not exceptional.

jokes.text: I wish her voice weren't so grating. Oh, that voice! Shrillary.
jokes.setup: Discussing Hillary Clinton in the context of the election
jokes.punchline: Lucille's catty nickname 'Shrillary'
jokes.explanation: Typical Lucille cattiness. The pun is obvious but fits her character perfectly.

jokes.text: God, who even remembers now? Saddam Hussein.
jokes.setup: George Sr. trying to downplay past business dealings
jokes.punchline: Lucille casually remembering they sold houses to Saddam Hussein
jokes.explanation: Excellent dark humor revealing the family's casual relationship with international crime. The matter-of-fact delivery is perfect.

jokes.text: It said, 'Welcome Home From Prison Again, Buster.' I mean, it's like, did they expect me to come home from prison?
jokes.setup: Buster noticing a banner meant for his homecoming
jokes.punchline: His oblivious interpretation of what was obviously planned for him
jokes.explanation: Good Buster obliviousness. His inability to understand that the banner was for him is perfectly in character.

jokes.text: A guy could have a mighty nice life up here. Do you... You want us to die here together?
jokes.setup: Oscar commenting positively about their attic hideout
jokes.punchline: Buster's dramatic misinterpretation as a death pact suggestion
jokes.explanation: Great misdirection where Buster jumps to the darkest possible interpretation. The contrast between comfort and death is excellent.

jokes.text: Impossible. You weren't even a wanted child.
jokes.setup: Dismissing the idea that Buster could be wanted by authorities
jokes.punchline: The brutal revelation that Buster wasn't wanted by his own family
jokes.explanation: Devastating one-liner that perfectly captures the family's cruelty. The wordplay between 'wanted criminal' and 'wanted child' is brilliant and cruel.

jokes.text: If... you help me get rid of this big, dead-body-shaped thing. What is it? I just told you, it's...
jokes.setup: Gob asking for help with a mysterious object
jokes.punchline: The implication that it's an actual dead body while being vague about it
jokes.explanation: Good dark humor setup. The vagueness about the body-shaped object while being obvious about it is well-done.

jokes.text: My name is Gob Bluth. I'm here for a 12:30 un-gaying.
jokes.setup: Gob arriving at conversion therapy
jokes.punchline: His casual, appointment-like approach to 'un-gaying'
jokes.explanation: Good character humor showing Gob's casual approach to serious topics. The made-up word 'un-gaying' is perfectly Gob.

jokes.text: Yeah. Boy, wish I could pray that sweater away.
jokes.setup: Gob at conversion therapy, supposedly learning to pray
jokes.punchline: His immediate focus on the therapist's fashion instead of the therapy
jokes.explanation: Perfect Gob moment - superficial and missing the point while being inadvertently funny. The prayer/fashion joke works well.

jokes.text: Oh, yeah. I... I noticed there's a blood-like substance back there. But that's a tomorrow job. That's for the daytime.
jokes.setup: Following up on the sticky truck stairs
jokes.punchline: The casual acknowledgment of blood evidence with a procrastination attitude
jokes.explanation: Excellent dark comedy with the matter-of-fact approach to cleaning up blood. The 'daytime job' attitude is perfectly casual.

tmdb_overview: As George Sr. attempts to win back Lucille, Michael assembles a mold of their scheme. Meanwhile, Buster gets caught up in Tobias and Gob's business.
insight: Cringe-heavy finale delivers 57 jokes across nested family chaos, just under elite threshold.
